5:
**Part 1 – Personal Biography**  

I’m 18 and spend most of my mornings cycling the 12 km from my parents’ house in the leafy suburbs of Pijnacker‑Nootdorp to the university campus in Delft, where I’m in the first year of a Biology bachelor. Between lectures I work a few evenings at the independent bookstore on the main square, shelving new releases and recommending titles to regulars. On weekends I help my neighbour, Mrs. de Vries, tend the community garden behind the local library; we grow tomatoes, kale, and a few experimental herb varieties.  

When I’m not in class or the garden, I’m on the field hockey pitch with my club, practising penalty corners and catching up with teammates over smoothies. Music is a constant companion—I’ve curated playlists of Dutch indie bands for my study sessions and love discovering new tracks on vinyl at the record shop down the road.  

Reading has always been a refuge; I’m currently finishing a biography of Rachel Carson and a collection of short stories by contemporary Dutch authors. My friends and I often meet at the café on the corner to debate the latest science podcasts, especially those about climate solutions and tech innovation. I value curiosity, reliability, and the sense that small, consistent actions can ripple into larger change.

---

**Part 2 – Political Perspective**  

My daily bike rides through the town’s bike lanes have shown me how essential well‑maintained, safe routes are for young people who rely on sustainable transport. When a pothole appeared near the school, the delay it caused for my classmates made me realize that infrastructure investment isn’t just about convenience—it directly affects our education and wellbeing.  

Working at the bookstore, I’ve seen how quickly publishers are adapting to digital formats, yet many students still struggle with the cost of textbooks. That experience drives my belief in policies that make higher education more affordable and promote open‑access resources.  

The community garden has taught me the power of local initiatives, but also the limits we face without broader support for urban agriculture and climate‑friendly policies. Seeing the impact of climate‑focused projects in my neighbourhood pushes me to back measures that accelerate the transition to renewable energy and protect green spaces.  

Finally, my university courses constantly highlight the importance of international collaboration in scientific research. I feel that a forward‑looking, globally engaged approach—especially within the EU—will give us the tools to tackle the challenges we’ll inherit. These lived experiences shape why I place my vote with a party that prioritises education, sustainability, and open‑society values.
